stem: Which body is primarily responsible for the prudential regulation of insurance companies in the UK?
explanation: The Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA), part of the Bank of England, is responsible for the prudential regulation and supervision of insurance companies, banks, and major investment firms.

stem: The Financial Conduct Authority is responsible for
options: regulating the conduct of all financial services firms and prudential regulation of firms not supervised by the PRA.
the regulation of pension schemes only.
explanation: The FCA regulates the conduct of all authorised financial services firms and is also the prudential regulator for firms not supervised by the PRA.

stem: Under FCA rules, a firm that wishes to carry on regulated activities must either be directly authorised or operate as
options: an appointed representative of an authorised firm.
explanation: Under FCA rules, firms must either hold direct authorisation or operate as an appointed representative of an authorised firm (the principal), which takes regulatory responsibility for the appointed representative's activities.

stem: Under the FCA's complaints handling rules (DISP), a firm must send a final response to a complainant within
explanation: Under the FCA's Dispute Resolution (DISP) rules, a firm must provide a final response to a complaint within 8 weeks. If unable to do so, the firm must explain why and inform the complainant of their right to refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

stem: The FCA's Consumer Duty requires authorised firms to act to deliver
explanation: The FCA's Consumer Duty requires authorised firms to act to deliver good outcomes for retail customers, raising the standard of care across financial services.

stem: The Insurance: Conduct of Business sourcebook (ICOBS) applies to
options: general insurance and pure protection contracts.
explanation: ICOBS sets out the FCA's conduct of business rules for firms carrying on insurance distribution activities relating to general insurance contracts and pure protection contracts.

stem: The Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS) provides protection to policyholders if an authorised insurer becomes
explanation: The FSCS is a fund of last resort that compensates eligible policyholders if an authorised insurer becomes insolvent and is unable to meet its obligations.

stem: For long-term insurance claims, the FSCS will pay compensation of
options: 50% of the claim with no upper limit.
90% of the claim with no upper limit.
100% of the claim with no upper limit.
explanation: For long-term insurance (including life assurance and income protection), the FSCS provides 100% protection of the claim with no upper limit.

stem: The ABI's Guide to Minimum Standards for group life schemes aims to promote
explanation: The ABI's Guide to Minimum Standards aims to promote consistency and clarity for customers by encouraging insurers to follow common standards for policy wordings and practices.

stem: The Group Risk Development (GRiD) organisation represents the interests of the
explanation: GRiD (Group Risk Development) is the industry body for the group risk protection sector, representing insurers, reinsurers, and intermediaries involved in group life, income protection, and critical illness.

stem: Under FCA rules, an intermediary must provide a client with an Initial Disclosure Document (IDD) at the earliest opportunity. The IDD must include details about the intermediary's
explanation: The IDD must include information about the intermediary's status, scope of service, and remuneration arrangements to ensure transparency for the client.

stem: A complainant who is dissatisfied with a firm's final response may refer the matter to the Financial Ombudsman Service within
explanation: A complainant has 6 months from the date of the firm's final response to refer their complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

stem: The Solvency II regime, as implemented in the UK, primarily governs
explanation: Solvency II (now adapted as the UK Solvency regime post-Brexit) sets out capital adequacy requirements for insurance companies to ensure they hold sufficient capital to meet their obligations.

stem: Under the FCA's rules, a financial promotion for a group risk product must be
options: approved by the Advertising Standards Authority.
explanation: The FCA requires that all financial promotions must be clear, fair and not misleading, ensuring customers are not given a false impression of the product.

stem: Which of the following is classified as a regulated activity by the FCA?
options: Advising on and arranging contracts of insurance.
Publishing general information about insurance products.
Supplying office equipment to insurance companies.
explanation: Advising on and arranging contracts of insurance are regulated activities under the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000. Firms carrying out these activities must be authorised by the FCA.

stem: The FCA categorises clients into three main types. A large corporate employer purchasing group risk insurance would most likely be classified as a
explanation: Under ICOBS, employers purchasing group insurance are typically classified as commercial customers. This is distinct from retail customers (individual consumers) and eligible counterparties (institutions).

stem: Under the FCA's Senior Managers and Certification Regime (SM&CR), senior managers at regulated firms are personally accountable for
options: all claims paid by the firm.
all profits generated by the firm.
the areas of the firm's business for which they are responsible.
the personal conduct of every employee.
explanation: Under SM&CR, senior managers are held personally accountable for the areas of the firm's business for which they are responsible, through a 'duty of responsibility' standard.

stem: The Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (FSMA) provides the legal framework for
options: financial services regulation in the UK.
explanation: FSMA 2000 is the primary legislation that provides the legal framework for financial services regulation in the UK, establishing the regulatory authorities and their powers.

stem: An insurer that fails to handle a claim fairly or promptly could face enforcement action from the
explanation: The FCA has the power to take enforcement action against authorised firms that fail to meet its conduct standards, including treating customers fairly in claims handling.

stem: The FCA's Conduct of Business sourcebook requires intermediaries to ensure that any personal recommendation for a group risk product is
options: approved by the employer's board of directors.
based on the cheapest available premium.
suitable for the client's demands and needs.
explanation: Under the FCA's conduct rules, intermediaries must ensure that any personal recommendation is suitable for the client's demands and needs, taking into account the client's circumstances and requirements.
